# Regression: `git checkout -- <sealed object>` restores an object without the
# audit log, so the on-disk seal root hash no longer matches the log. A normal
# (full) verify fails on that, but the post-checkout hook must tolerate it - it
# loads with no-disk verification and reveals from the checked-out object rather
# than aborting the checkout with a root-hash error.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
cp secret.env app.env
exec sesam add app.env --group admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'v1 of the secret'

# Rotate + re-seal the secret: new object and a new seal root hash (uncommitted).
cp secret2.env app.env
exec sesam seal

# Restore ONLY the sealed object from HEAD - it now disagrees with the (v2) log.
exec git checkout HEAD -- .sesam/objects/app.env.sesam

# A normal full verify fails on the on-disk root-hash mismatch…
! exec sesam verify
stderr 'root hash mismatch'

# …but the post-checkout hook loads with no-disk verify and reveals the
# checked-out (v1) object instead of aborting.
rm app.env
exec sesam hook post-checkout HEAD HEAD 0
exists app.env
grep 'super-secret' app.env

-- secret.env --
TOKEN=super-secret
-- secret2.env --
TOKEN=rotated-secret
